# Transcrição: 08-Reduce CPM using The VCO Strategy (Variable Campaign Objectives)

## [00:00 - 03:00]

oceans, boats and hooks, the real way that Facebook actually works. I hear from people talking about their audience sizes and they've got 1 million, 5 million, 15 million people in their audience and sometimes they don't understand why they're not able to get stability or they're scaling up and their roast is dropping and they're not able to bring their roast back and other things like if there's a million in my audience that's potentially a million buyers that is false and I want to show you what that actually means in context of how Facebook actually works. So you know understanding this is like taking the analogy of a fishing boat. So let's just say that you take your fishing boat out into a large ocean and you have an idea of where your fish are going to be and where they are and you've been directed into that area. Now what you're doing is you know you found this big area of the ocean that you believe that your kind of best fish are and then you're sending, you're going out with your boat and you're trying to catch the fish and you're throwing the hook in. You may or may not get the fish that you're looking for. So first of all, there may be fish but it might not be the right fish. Secondly, it could be that you've got some fish but not enough or of course you may catch the exact fish that you're looking for but there's not as many as you thought there were and the way I like to share this analogy when it comes to Facebook is literally the kind of big circle of the ocean that you go into is your audience. So your audience is defined by your targeting, your ad set targeting, your placements, your demographics, your lookalikes, your interests. That's defining this big ocean of Facebook-ness your audiences are. So your audience size you know could be 10 million so this big net that you've created is 10 million prospective people. Your campaign objective is guiding your ad set as to where to find those fish and what type of fish to look for. So for example, in the Facebook world if you set your campaign objective to website conversion and then you say you know I want people that are likely to purchase you're narrowing that field down so the audience number is no longer 10 million because not all 10 million people in there are likely to buy or you know even even click your ad and that's kind of setting the direction for the ad set then the ads are literally the hooks that you're dropping in for the fish and this is why I talk about angles and hooks and messages as part of the graduation testing process and I'm kind of almost testing the different hooks out so you know thinking about the hook analogy in a fishing boat for the different audiences. So for example if I drop this net this hook here and you

know I've got my ad set and the fish don't bite then it either means that I'm fishing in the wrong area or my hook is wrong and the same analogy applies for your ad sets and ads so when you go through the grad testing process and you find that an ad does not work it doesn't mean that it's a bad ad it could be that it's the wrong audience or conversely if you define an audience and the ad doesn't ## [06:00 - 09:03]

audience size is huge it's 50 million it's 20 million whatever you've got so much scope to scale up the reality could be that you've maxed out your potential and your overreaching your audience so this is now when you start looking at your ad quality scores you start looking at auction insights to see what else could be going wrong because if you are at the at the position where you're scaling significantly but you're seeing a diminishing return so your return on ad spend is starting to drop your cost per acquisition starting to increase but in your mind you think there's still so much room to scale up it could just be that you've overreached the core audience in that ad set so the core audience for you could be all the purchases so here's the thing now if you're in that position there's a few things you can do number one is to obviously increase the size of the audience so from 10 million to 10 to 20 million if you've got that scope it could be by increasing your lookalikes could be by going broad maybe trying interests for example or you could try a different audience and try and find a different pocket of users but one of the methods that people I think don't realize is when Facebook looks at any given audience it's looking at the likelihood of someone taking a certain action based on the history so if you've purchased before Facebook will mark you as a purchaser if you purchase recently then they'll mark you as someone that's potentially going to buy again soon if you've bought in apparel then they'll mark you as a person that's bought an apparel product so now you can start getting an idea of the data that Facebook's collecting on you as an individual so that when the advertiser comes along and says you know I'm running an apparel store I'm going to target these people here's my apparel ad etc Facebook's now going through its list of people who are in the purchase audience and saying right who's purchased recently who's purchased at any time who's purchased apparel and they're trying to build this picture of who your ideal prospect is the other thing about this is you know let's say for example not only have you purchased and do you purchase online but you may also click you may also engage you may also view videos so there's going to be a big overlap so for example if you view videos and you click and you engage in ads then you know as a purchaser you're kind of in any of these smaller segments so here this part here is pure purchases this part here is people that purchased and viewed videos and then you can kind of start seeing the smaller pockets within that then you look here and you say right people who click but don't purchase these are the people here people that click and engage etc etc and there's all these kind of small pockets of audiences that going on one of the things that I've seen when I've started to scale up is when you start looking at other objectives you can often reach other parts of your audience which Facebook may not have

## [09:03 - 12:04]

recognized as being purchasers but they may be purchasers and it's something that I test at the highest scale level so for example if I'm looking at a website conversion campaign and you know I've optimized for purchasers I might expand that out and make that audience size bigger by now targeting people who have added to cart now you might look at that and say well that's just window shoppers they're not necessarily going to buy and that may be true but until you test it you may otherwise exhaust an audience before you're ready to or for example I might run my website conversion campaign and introduce a video view campaign alongside that if I have a video and run them both together it has a twofold benefit number one I'm running a video so I'm getting lower costs and I'm able to reach more people and secondly I'm increasing my pot of people to hit my second layer of my full funnel system so I can remark it to them as well and these could be for example you know here's something to bear in mind just because Facebook has not recognized someone as a purchaser based on their history or recency they haven't purchased doesn't mean they won't there are many people out there that Facebook don't know whether they're going to buy or not and if you can reach them chances are your cost is going to be lower because guess where the most expensive audiences are it's the people that everyone's going after so you know if you go off if you if you go into any training program on Facebook ads you go to Facebook's own program you know they will point you towards targeting purchasers so set up a website conversion campaign and set your event to purchase but not many people realize that especially at the larger scales and you know partly it's when you're you're spending large amounts on your adsets and your campaigns but secondly it's also when you're in a situation where you're in a country where your audience sizes are small so for example if you're in a 1% lookalike in the US you're talking about a couple of million people right right there if you're in a smaller country your 1% lookalike might even be 200,000 or three or four or 500,000 and if that's the size of your total audience and you're just going after purchasers that's not a lot of people but you can expand that out beyond the data that Facebook has by testing different objectives so for example you might combine website purchases with clickers now clickers are people that are likely to click into your site but maybe you don't have purchase history I would personally do that as a website conversion combined with add to cart or view content depending on if it's e-commerce or info products rather than the link clicks campaign that's just something which is based on pixel data on the site so for example when you run a campaign which is website conversion and you've got the pixel installed on your landing page for view content then that is a positive signal you can also add

## [12:04 - 15:06]

additional layers to that by looking at delaying your pixel event so the view content pixel fire could happen after 20 seconds after 30 seconds making sure obviously your pixel has fired but then you delay the event so the the quality of the data you're picking up there is higher so you can create lookalikes from that you can expand that out for example you might do purchases with video viewers you might do purchases with engagers this is something often we do and in fact one of the accounts we're running right now we are running the website conversion campaigns we're doing about five maybe eight k a day on ad spend and we're also using our page post engagement campaigns for social proofing so if you've been through grad testing you'll understand that we often take our winning ads from grad testing and we push them into a social proof campaign which is page post engagement with the goal of getting comments and ads and reusing that for our prospecting but often we see sales come through there as well now it's not to the extent that we can scale it up because page post engagement campaigns are not designed for scaling and very rarely do they scale up with spend but we're actually seeing positive return on ad spend in our in our social proofing campaigns which is really really fascinating because they're not designed by us to generate sales they're just there for social proof and and Facebook have identified people in that audience as people that are engaged but they're also buying and and and it's because of this there will be people in that audience which we're reaching for as far cheaper that are also purchasers and we're able to secure that sale cheaper than just going off to purchasers so it's just a fascinating way that the auction's being built up and the understanding that people have or the different pots of users and what the reality is as well and also bear in mind people are always flicking between different things as well so you know you may you may have bought something 30 days ago and maybe you haven't bought anything since but you're you know you might be fading out of the kind of purchasers pot here but you are still viewing videos because you enjoy that you're engaging in content and stuff like that you might have drifted away from that purchasers pot and you're just sitting here it doesn't mean you won't buy again it doesn't mean that you're not a buyer but it means that Facebook have not identified you as a purchaser because their goal is to find you the best users for the best price best conversion etc as long as you're given the best signals our goal as an advertiser is to manipulate that in in our favor as much as possible to balance cost scaling and reach as well so going back to our example of the fishing boats this is kind of how i see it working so you have multiple ad sets which are your fishing boats they settle in different parts of your auction then the ads are literally the hooks dropping in so even if you've got a big

## [15:06 - 16:22]

pool of potential customers and you've got different ad sets that are in there and targeting different people it's it's it's how the auction works that you may or may not find the right people at the right time and it's the goal of your optimization to shift your ad sets into the right areas so for example if your ad set is not performing here and and you know you might say that my ads are not working quite often is the ad set is collecting bad data because those ads have dropped into the wrong part of the auction sometimes you pause the ads and start them again and the ad set drifts into the right area sometimes you duplicate the ad set reset the data and start again and then the ad set drops into the right part of the auction as well and then you know when you're looking across multiple campaigns this this is kind of the the high level 38 000 foot view of everything that's going on is what we've just discussed is that the kind of micro and macro level but this is happening all the way across your campaigns your ad sets and ads so it's really important when your audience size is small and your or your spend is increasing to consider how you're looking at your objectives so that you can maximize your performance reduce your costs and increase your profitability as well as your stability
